title: Revealing the deep structure and rupture plane of the 2010 Maule, Chile earthquake (Mw = 8.8) using wide angle seismic data
abstract: The February 27, 2010 Maule earthquake ruptured ~400 km of the Nazca–South America plate boundary. Seismic refraction data reveal the fore-arc structure and subduction zone characteristics of the rupture region. A ~40 km wide accretionary prism is identified and the backstop location marking the seismogenic zone's updip boundary is determined using marine seismic reflections. Wide-angle P-wave data from multiple OBS profiles across the Maule earthquake rupture zone (34°–36°S) are used to constrain the velocity structure and geometry of the subducting Nazca plate and the seismogenic zone.

location.description: Offshore central Chile between 34°S and 36°S, Maule earthquake (Mw 8.8, 2010) rupture zone

data.name: Wide-angle OBS seismic refraction profiles, Maule earthquake rupture zone (34°–36°S)
data.description: Wide-angle OBS seismic refraction data from multiple profiles across the 2010 Maule earthquake rupture zone (34°–36°S). Used for P-wave tomography of the seismogenic zone structure.

processing.summary: Wide-angle OBS seismic refraction data from multiple profiles across the 2010 Maule earthquake rupture zone (34°–36°S) processed to produce 2D P-wave velocity models of the subduction zone. Travel time tomography used to constrain fore-arc structure, accretionary prism width (~40 km), backstop geometry, and subducting Nazca plate dip. Marine seismic reflection data also used to identify the backstop. Results provide velocity and structural constraints on the seismogenic zone updip limit.
workflow: 1. OBS deployment along multiple trench-perpendicular profiles
7. Integration with 2010 Maule earthquake aftershock seismicity
processing.notes: Post-Maule earthquake rapid response survey. Data acquired in 2010 offshore south-central Chile.

analysis_notes: Paper behind paywall; no open access version found. Abstract reconstructed from survey snippet. Acquisition year estimated as 2010 (post-Maule earthquake rapid response). Grevemeyer and Flueh (GEOMAR) suggest R/V SONNE. Rabbel and Thorwart (Kiel University) co-authorship confirms German collaboration.
